title: Dundee cake
subtitle: The Scottish light fruit cake with a top of concentric almonds
excerpt: A buttery Scottish fruit cake, lighter than a Christmas cake, fragrant with orange and lemon zest and packed with currants, sultanas, and peel. The defining feature is the pattern of whole blanched almonds pressed in rings across the top.
sourceNotes: Dundee cake is a nineteenth-century Scottish confection documented by Keiller's of Dundee, the marmalade manufacturers, who developed the recipe to use surplus orange peel. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives a fruit cake consistent with the Dundee style. The concentric almond top is the universally recognised visual signature of the cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the cooled cake in baking paper and foil and freeze. Thaw at room temperature for several hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for two weeks in an airtight tin; it improves after the first three days as the fruit softens and the flavour develops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-mixing technique in which butter and sugar are beaten together until pale and fluffy before eggs and flour are added. Incorporates air and produces a tender, even crumb.

content.text: Dundee cake is a lighter, less dense alternative to a Christmas or wedding cake. It is made by the

rather than by boiling or soaking the fruit, which gives a less compressed crumb. The fruit content is lower than a full Christmas cake: currants and sultanas dominate, with lemon and orange zest in the batter. Whisky or brandy can be added but is not traditional in the Dundee version.

The top is covered in concentric rings of whole blanched almonds pressed in before baking. They do not sink; if set carefully, they remain upright and toast to a deep gold by the end of the bake. A sheen of beaten egg brushed over the almonds halfway through gives them a glossy surface.

attrs.prepNote: large; one lightly beaten for glazing the almonds

content.text: Heat the oven to 150°C fan (170°C conventional). Double-line the base and sides of a 20 cm round cake tin with baking paper. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{light-brown-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in three of the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{plain-flour}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{baking-powder}}. Stir in {{currants}}, {{sultanas}}, and the zests from {{orange}} and {{lemon}}.
Add the almond top and bake

Spoon the batter into the tin and level the surface. Arrange the {{blanched-almonds}} in concentric rings across the top, pressing each one in gently and upright. Bake for 60 minutes, then brush the almond top with the remaining beaten {{eggs}}. Return to the oven for a further 35–40 minutes until a skewer comes out clean and the almonds are toasted. The total bake time is about 95–100 minutes; an oven thermometer helps avoid the outside over-baking while the centre lags. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then on a rack.
If the almonds begin to colour too much before the cake is done, lay a sheet of foil loosely over the top without pressing it down.
The cake is best eaten three days after baking. It slices cleanly by then and the fruit has had time to settle into the crumb.
A tablespoon of Scotch whisky folded into the finished batter before baking is a popular variation, though it is not part of the original Keiller recipe.
